slides.lead: Demo presentation of the template. All text is sample (dummy) copy and only serves to show the styles.
slides.notes: Opening slide. Put the event name, a one-line subtitle and a meta row at the bottom here. Highlight one word with em for the accent color.

slides.lead: Five roles, one shared vision of place.
slides.notes: Team slide. Instead of photos it uses initials in a colored circle, so it needs no images.

slides.lead: Three parts, with room for questions between them.
slides.notes: Agenda as three large tiles. Good at the start of a block.

slides.lead: Site, program and the constraints that shape the design.
slides.notes: Section divider between blocks. Large heading on a colored gradient.

slides.lead: Sun, wind, street and neighbors set the rules before a single line is drawn. The design answers them.
slides.footnote: // illustrative slide with an enlarged image
slides.notes: Hero slide: large heading, bold illustration, short caption. Good for a key point or a chapter opener.

items: lower operating energy after passive design measures
saved on approvals with an early code review
more usable floor area from a smarter core layout
bullets: Measure outcomes, not effort: what changes for the user wins.
Start where the constraints are tightest and the value is clearest.
A small studied option beats a grand scheme with no metric.
slides.notes: KPI dashboard: three cards with an icon, a large number and a label, with supporting bullets below.

slides.lead: We test many ideas cheaply before committing to one.
bullets: The site, the climate and the daily routines of users.
Dozens of rough massing options, fast and loose.
Each option against light, cost and the brief.
To the one scheme worth detailing further.
slides.notes: Two-column slide: bullets with a strong lead word on the left, illustration on the right. Text scales itself.

slides.lead: Same plot, two approaches and two very different costs.
cards: Keep the shell, rework the inside. Lower carbon, more surprises.
Clear and start fresh. Clean, but slow and costly.
Add to what stands. A middle path that often fits the budget.
Keep the strong parts, replace the weak ones. Case by case.
slides.notes: A 2x2 card grid. Good for comparison or listing variants.

slides.lead: Six building blocks of a studio project.
cards: The clear written goal every drawing answers to.
Rough volumes that test fit and form early.
Orientation and shade before any machines.
One coordinated model the whole team shares.
Catching rules early, before they cost time.
The builders who turn drawings into rooms.
slides.notes: A 3x2 grid with an icon in each card header. Great for an overview of tools or capabilities.

items: The neighborhood settles and the design stays as built.
Interest rises fast and the plan must leave room to expand.
The building learns new uses well beyond its first brief.
slides.notes: Numbered cards with small trend charts and an illustration on the right. Ideal for three possible futures.

slides.lead: Five stages, each with a clear deliverable.
slides.notes: A horizontal flow of stages with arrows. Each box has a title and a small caption.

slides.quote: We shape our buildings, and afterwards our buildings shape us.
slides.by: Sample quote to show the style
slides.notes: Quote slide with a bold left rule. Keep the attribution small.

slides.lead: Simple, but easy to forget under pressure.
bullets: Every decision has the name of a person behind it.
You know up front what the project can afford.
On the choices that matter, the client decides.
slides.notes: A plain bullet slide with a strong lead word. Text scales up automatically to fill the space.

slides.lead: A site with no limits tends to produce a design with no idea.
slides.notes: Statement slide: one large sentence centered on the page. Use it for a key message.

slides.lead: What happens this week, this month and this quarter.
slides.notes: Second divider, opening the practical part.

slides.lead: We switch to the tool and walk through the building live.
slides.footnote: // placeholder slide for a demo or a break
slides.notes: Placeholder slide. Centered heading, ideal for a live demo, a break or questions.
Closing slide in the style of the title. Contact and a call to action.
